sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Wound closure reduces bleeding, protects from contamination, and promotes healing. Prehospital suturing is appropriate for clean, simple lacerations where transport time is prolonged. Steri-strips (adhesive wound closure strips) are suitable for superficial, low-tension lacerations.

sections.title: Steri-Strip Application
sections.content:
1. Ensure wound is clean and haemostasis achieved; dry wound edges

2. Approximate wound edges manually

3. Apply Steri-strip across wound: half on each side perpendicular to the wound

4. Start at centre; work toward each end alternating sides

5. Apply parallel strips 3–4 mm apart

6. Apply bridging strip along wound edges for extra support if required

sections.title: Simple Interrupted Suturing
sections.content:
1. Achieve haemostasis; irrigate wound; consider local anaesthetic (lignocaine 1%)

2. Select appropriate suture: 4-0 nylon for face; 3-0 nylon for scalp/body; 2-0 for high tension

3. Load needle driver with needle; grasp at 2/3 from tip

4. Enter skin 3–5 mm from wound edge at 90°; pass through to opposite side equidistant

5. Tie square knot: instrument tie technique (3 throws: 2 forward + 1 reverse)

6. Cut tails 4–5 mm from knot

7. Space sutures: 4–5 mm apart for face; 8–10 mm for scalp/body

8. Document: site, number of sutures, suture material used, date

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Do not close: contaminated wounds, bites, wounds > 6–8 hours old
- Always irrigate before closure
- Deep sutures for deep tissue tension before skin closure
- Remove sutures: face 3–5 days; scalp 7–10 days; body 7–14 days
